name: Lamb shift and bound-state QED
requirement: Any candidate framework must reproduce the hydrogen 2S-2P Lamb shift, currently measured to ~kHz precision (~10^-6 fractional) and consistent with bound-state QED. The Bezginov et al. 2019 measurement of the 2S_1/2 to 2P_1/2 splitting in hydrogen, combined with muonic-hydrogen Lamb-shift measurements (Pohl et al. 2010), simultaneously determines the proton charge radius and tests QED.

regime.description: Hydrogen 2S-2P fine-structure splitting; sensitive to QED vacuum polarization and self-energy at the alpha^5 m_e c^2 level.

current_bound.experiment: Frequency-comb hydrogen spectroscopy at York University
current_bound.reference: Bezginov, Valdez, Horbatsch, Marsman, Vutha, Hessels, 'A measurement of the atomic hydrogen Lamb shift and the proton charge radius', Science 365, 1007 (2019)

what_it_excludes: Any modification of QED vacuum polarization, electron self-energy, or proton charge structure at fractional precision > 10^-6 is excluded. Bound-state QED tested at four-loop accuracy. Together with muonic hydrogen, definitively settled the proton-radius puzzle by 2019: r_p ~ 0.842 fm.

parameterization.name: Bound-state QED with one free parameter (proton radius), plus any framework-specific modifications to the Coulomb potential or photon propagator.
parameterization.reference: Karshenboim, Phys. Rep. 422, 1 (2005); Pohl et al., Nature 466, 213 (2010) (muonic hydrogen).
parameterization.summary: Lamb-shift theory expansion in alpha and (Z alpha) m_e/m_p with calculable coefficients up to four-loop level.

procedural.predicted_observable: Delta E_LS = sum_n a_n alpha^(n+1) (Z alpha)^m R_infinity + nuclear-structure corrections proportional to <r_p^2>.
framework_inputs: Any modification to the photon propagator at energies <~ atomic scale, or any new electron-photon coupling, must be evaluated for its contribution to the 2S-2P shift.

primary_references.citation: Bezginov et al., 'A measurement of the atomic hydrogen Lamb shift and the proton charge radius', Science 365, 1007 (2019)
Pohl et al., 'The size of the proton', Nature 466, 213 (2010)
Karshenboim, 'Precision Physics of Simple Atoms: QED Tests, Nuclear Structure and Fundamental Constants', Phys. Rep. 422, 1 (2005)
